data.title: GoodRx: Novo Deal And TrumpRx Talks Point To Ebitda/FCF Upside
data.description:
Introduction

GoodRx Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:GDRX) stock popped then dropped following a report that it plans to join TrumpRx.

In August, the company joined Novo Nordisk (NVO) to offer GoodRx customers access to its popular weight loss drug, Wegovy, for $499/month out-of-pocket. This comes as both Eli Lilly (LLY) and Novo Nordisk are working to increase access to their GLP-1s for self-pay patients amid inconsistent insurance coverage. For instance, both pharma companies sell their GLPs directly to consumers for ~$499/month.

There is a clear divergence between what GoodRx stands for (expanding access and helping Americans save on medicines) and these recent developments. In the article that follows, I propose that GoodRx appears poised to seize these opportunities as leverage to improve its fundamentals.

Business Overview

Because this is my first analysis on GoodRx (although I have some existing familiarity), I want to take a moment to describe what they do and how they make money.

GoodRx operates a consumer-focused digital healthcare platform. It aggregates prescription drug prices and provides discount codes. Its core service is free for users. I have used a discount coupon in the past.

How does GoodRx make money doing this? When a consumer uses a GoodRx code at the pharmacy, the PBM pays GoodRx a fee (aka “transaction revenue”) for directing the prescription fill (~70% of revenues). The company also generates revenue from pharma manufacturers (ads and sponsorships of discount programs, ~17% of revenue) and consumer subscriptions (GoodRx Gold fees, ~10% of revenue).

This structure means that economics are mainly volume-driven. And because GoodRx incurs minimal direct costs on each transaction, gross margins are consistently high (>90%).

Recent Trends

With that said, GoodRx revenues have stagnated somewhat. In its Q2 2025 report, revenues increased just 1% Y/Y to $203.1 million. While this was a slight beat of internal guidance (flat QoQ), it missed Street expectations.

The growth was thanks to Pharma Solutions' revenue (ads/point-of-sale programs for pharma), which grew 32% Y/Y to $35 million. As usual, gross margins were high (93.4%). Although this was a slight drop due to the mix (higher telehealth and program costs). GoodRx’s adjusted EBITDA was $69.4 million. Free cash flow was $49.2 million (24% margin). Net income was $12.8 million.

GoodRx’s recent issues can be tied to a material decline in Monthly Active Consumers (MACs). This dropped 14% Y/Y from 6.6M to 5.7M. The company attributes this to retail pharmacy disruptions (closures) and the end of a large partner program. That being said, GoodRx retains a leading share of the U.S. prescription discount market despite formidable competition due to its strong brand and scale. GoodRx’s subscription plans are dropping, too (778k in Q124 → 680k in Q125). This was attributed to the sunset of the Kroger subscription program.

Despite the 14% drop in MAC, GoodRx has been able to somewhat offset this by improving average revenue per user, or ARPU. So, in Q2, prescription revenue fell only 3%. We call this “improved unit economics” in business school. The company is getting a higher take rate per transaction.

Valuation Assumptions

Assuming a base case of 2.5% Y/Y revenue growth and 16% FCF margins (see Appendix for model assumptions), GDRX appears only modestly undervalued.

"Modestly undervalued" checks out with other metrics:

Modeling GDRX on a cash flow basis was unsurprisingly easy. 1) “Easy” in that it required little tinkering, and 2) “unsurprising” because its stock price is essentially unchanged since Fall 2022 (which implies that its growth/margin assumptions are unchanged).

This does not happen often when I model, but margins and growth carry the same weight for GDRX.

In other words, a 1pp improvement in either margin or growth results in a $0.33 stock price improvement. So, for GoodRx, improving growth is just as important as improving its margins.

Finally, I ran a Monte Carlo simulation on my main DCF function to account for some variability among key inputs like growth, beta, and margins.

Uncoincidentally, GDRX's 52-week range ($3.31-$7.11) is near my model’s 5th and 95th percentiles. These percentiles basically tell us what GDRX could trade between (assuming the base case) without becoming unreasonably over- or undervalued.

As for scenario comparison, I compared my base case with a bullish and bearish outcome.

The bearish one (ongoing Rx-Transaction Pressure) speaks for itself. In the following section, I discuss why I believe Novo/TrumpRx Mix Lift can meaningfully impact GDRX’s valuation if the developments materialize in earnings.

Novo/TrumpRx Lift

GoodRx’s Novo Nordisk collaboration is a direct volume catalyst in Pharma Manufacturer Solutions via branded-drug access programs. Recall that this segment grew 32% Y/Y in Q2. It makes good sense, right? Pharma companies like Novo want to increase access to patients. GoodRx’s 5.7M MACs incentivize collaborations. The collaboration could also indirectly lift Prescription Transactions revenue. If GLP-1 seekers discover GoodRx, they may then use traditional coupons for non-GLP-1 generic meds like antihypertensives and statins. I think that if onboarding/retargeting is tight, this could result in a 1-3% MAC tailwind. GDRX’s initial response to the Novo partnership (~30% increase in early morning trading) may have been the market pricing this in. GDRX price soon faded as investor sentiment is poor and likely in a “prove it” mode.

GoodRx’s potential role in TrumpRx.gov (WH-backed DTC portal) is more uncertain (the site hasn’t launched yet, and GoodRx is in talks to participate). GoodRx could help orchestrate access (think eligibility, benefit checks, coupon/voucher generation) and offer manufacturer solutions (traffic acquisition, program enrollment). If GoodRx becomes a preferred “access partner” listed on TrumpRx brand pages, it could meaningfully lift GoodRx’s Pharma Manufacturer Solutions segment. Prescription Transactions could also benefit from a limited direct lift.

But why GoodRx? I think a neutral, multi-branded access hub is attractive to manufacturers and retailers seeking scale. Moreover, GoodRx’s existing counter integrations position it as a reliable bridge.

Growth Levers

In addition to Novo/TrumpRx opportunities, GoodRx has a number of growth and margin levers to pull.

Starting with growth levers:

Final Thoughts

While Novo/TrumpRx collaborations provide some upside potential, GDRX’s valuation strikes me as appropriate. Despite recent operating leverage (increasing adjusted EBITDA on flat Y/Y revenues), the KPIs (MACs, subscriptions) are concerning. Now, the Novo collaboration can help stabilize these numbers, but if you’re expecting a “turnaround,” you may need to check yourself.

I didn’t touch on it (it is well-known), but competitors like SingleCare, Optum Perks, Amazon Pharmacy (AMZN), and Mark Cuban Cost Plus Drug Company are formidable competitors. While many are smaller (SingleCare’s pharmacy network is about half the size of GoodRx’s) or largely target submarkets (Cost Plus only carries ~1,000 generics; focused on mail-order generics), they are, collectively, still taking share from GoodRx.

The takeaway? Novo plus potential TrumpRx don’t “fix” GoodRx’s shrinking user base. They do shift the mix toward higher-margin manufacturer solutions, which should lift near-term EBITDA/FCF. And as I alluded to earlier, core coupon transactions could see a small indirect life as well.

In the coming quarters, I am most interested to see if GLP-1 funnel traffic will result in MAC stabilization. Once this is established, I think there is a lot of upside potential, as evidenced by my scenario comparison. To get ahead of this real possibility, I'd rate GDRX as a speculative "Buy," but this is in the context of a barbell portfolio (with limited allocation).

Appendix

DCF model inputs assumptions:

  • Year 1 Revenue: $798.9 million—the consensus analyst estimates for FY25 and is in line with management guidance.
  • Revenue Growth: 2.5%—based on recent trends and future estimates.
  • FCF Margin: 16%—on a GAAP OCF-CapEx basis; Q/Q swings due to timing of collections; this is a mid-cycle estimate.
  • Beta: 1.23—based on market data; consistent with industry
  • Interest-Bearing Debt, Cash & Equivalents (includes ST investments), and Cost of Debt—based on financial data.

Everything else is standard DCF modeling methodology.

data.title: SoFi Vs. Upstart: Which Fintech To Choose And For Whom
data.description:
Introduction

I decided to make today a comparison of two well-known and popular stocks among retail investors – SoFi Technologies, Inc. (NASDAQ:SOFI) and Upstart Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:UPST). You might argue there are no grounds to compare these two stocks because they have little in common besides both being a part of the U.S. fintech industry. But I found there was more resemblance, at least until recently. Looking at the graph below, you would see that both stocks were moving almost identically for a very long time. If one rallied, another rallied as well, and the opposite is also true. All of these moves were related to the U.S. economy and industry tailwinds or headwinds. But recently, they went two different ways: SoFi is rallying, while Upstart is plunging.

That divergence is interesting, and I think it says a lot about how investors now view these companies. It also shows how different their realities have become, even if they still technically belong to the same sector. At the end of this article, I would like to answer whether any of these stocks is a compelling buying opportunity and for what type of investor.

Macro Environment

First of all, I have to mention the U.S. economy, as it directly impacts the stock market, especially the financial sector. We are now in one of the strangest macro environments possible. The United States still faces recession risks, and consumer sentiment is still fragile due to unemployment and labor situation, but the Federal Reserve has already started cutting rates. So, we have this very confusing moment where rate cuts should, in theory, support both companies, but a recession would do the exact opposite. There is obviously no way to predict what exactly would happen, but there is a way to adjust to these conditions using your assumptions.

If the economy manages to stabilize without falling into a downturn, both SoFi and Upstart could continue to perform very well (probably better than now) because they will benefit from cheaper funding and a healthier lending environment. But if the Fed ends up cutting rates precisely because the economy is weakening, the effect could be negative for both.

Revenue Growth

If we look at the past few years, SoFi’s growth has been very consistent. Since 2018, its revenue has increased from roughly 300 million dollars to around 3 billion, which means it grew almost tenfold in about seven years. Since January 2023 until today, its revenue has increased by 132%, which was a little slower than before, but it was expected, as the bigger the company is, the harder it is to grow. In the latest quarter, SoFi reported record adjusted net revenue of 858 million dollars, which was up 44% year over year. I have been following SoFi stock for about a year and a half, and to tell you honestly, looking back, I wouldn’t have believed they would be getting to the $1 billion per quarter in revenue territory in 2025. The company is doing many things right, and it doesn't look like the growth will ever stop.

Upstart is different. Its revenue went from about 100 million dollars in 2017 to ~900 million today, which represents a higher compound growth rate, but it hasn’t been steady. The company experienced huge growth during the pandemic when credit conditions were loose, and then revenue collapsed when funding pulled back in 2022. After nearly three years of revenue shrinking, it returned to growth a year ago, and in the latest quarter, the growth is at an impressive 102% year-over-year.

The Business Models

Even though both companies operate in the same financial technology space, their models are almost opposite.

SoFi is now a diversified digital bank. It truly became a “one stop-shop” for people’s finances, as the management had promised it would be. SoFi gets deposits from customers, provides high-yield accounts, originates and sells loans, runs an investment platform, issues credit cards, licenses its technology to other financial institutions, opens trading and crypto platforms, and has huge plans to expand that even further. It has been working incredibly for them, as this way, they can do cross-selling and keep customers inside their ecosystem. It means they can grow without paying for the addition of new users, which is a great skill for a company in a VERY competitive landscape.

Upstart is completely different. It’s technological, and it doesn’t try to be a bank. It uses artificial intelligence to assess credit risk and connects borrowers with lenders. I have a full breakdown of Upstart from May and a SoFi breakdown from March. Upstart wants to be a platform that helps other institutions make better lending decisions without having to put the loans on their balance sheet. SoFi also refinances loans, but not with AI, and another difference is that UPST focuses solely on that, and SoFi doesn’t.

In general, the idea of loan refinancing is brilliant, but the implementation for Upstart has been difficult. When the Fed began raising rates and fears of a recession increased, banks and credit unions stopped buying loans from Upstart’s platform. To keep the business running, Upstart had to fund part of those loans itself, which is exactly what it didn’t want to do, but it didn’t have another choice.

Now that rates are coming down, this pressure is easing. The company originated 2.8 billion dollars in loans last quarter, which was a 154% increase year over year, and reported positive net income of 6 million dollars. That’s an impressive rebound, but I wouldn’t say the dark days are totally behind us. Upstart’s long-term performance depends on whether large lenders will truly adopt its AI-based model instead of the traditional FICO-score-driven approach.

SoFi funds its operations with deposits, and by now, almost 90% of its funding base comes from its own customers, which is impressive for a young fintech. Deposits reached nearly 30 billion dollars in the second quarter, which gives SoFi a significant advantage in cost of capital. The company can either hold loans and earn interest or sell them through its new Loan Platform Business, which already produces around half a billion dollars in annualized, fee-based revenue.

So it’s clear that SoFi is more independent, which makes Upstart a riskier play, but it could also be an opportunity if things move in a positive direction.

Profitability

Upstart operates like a software company, and when it works well, its margins can be spectacular. In the second quarter, its contribution margin was 58%, and its adjusted EBITDA margin was 21%. That’s the power of a platform model. When the fixed costs are covered, every additional loan adds profit immediately.

SoFi’s margins are naturally lower because it is a bank, and banking is a more capital-intensive business. Even so, SoFi’s adjusted EBITDA margin of 29% is impressive for a company that keeps expanding so quickly. It has now been profitable for seven consecutive quarters and continues to grow its tangible book value, which reached over 5.3 billion dollars in the second quarter.

Valuation

The biggest gap for these two stocks appears in valuation, and SoFi is way ahead. SoFi trades much higher than Upstart. Moreover, on most metrics, SoFi is trading close to its most expensive levels in history.

Upstart’s valuation is the complete opposite. Its forward price-to-earnings ratio is about 26, one of the lowest multiples it has ever traded at. The stock was left behind because investors are still cautious about whether the AI-based lending model can scale in a weak macro environment.

If the economy continues to stabilize and credit conditions remain benign, Upstart could have much larger upside simply because expectations are so low. But if macro conditions worsen or consumer delinquencies rise, the fall is likely to be even stronger than for SoFi, because once again, SoFi is more independent.

Conclusion

Both companies represent a modern approach to finance, but they are very different in their core. But if you were looking for a fintech stock, you were probably considering them both. Both are great companies that could have a solid future, but it looks like they are in completely different phases of their development. SoFi looks much better than UPST does right now. At the same time, SoFi hasn’t always looked as appealing as it does right now. Their road has been bumpy as well, and dozens of risks could have ruined this growth trajectory.

Today, SoFi is becoming a digital version of a universal bank. It has deposits, lending, and investing. It also benefits from a clear competitive moat: once customers join, it becomes increasingly easy for SoFi to sell them additional services. The company reported almost 900 million dollars in quarterly revenue, and the momentum is incredibly strong. However, after such an enormous rally, the stock already reflects a lot of the success.

The stock could rerate after the earnings, and speaking of this month (October), earnings (in less than 3 weeks) will probably be a catalyst from today’s level. But still, the valuation leaves little room for mistakes. If you really like the company, you could start a position and DCA it as time goes by. I think if we are talking about a long term of three or more years, this could look like a compelling buying opportunity.

Upstart is more complicated. It’s still small, it’s very volatile, and it's far more dependent on the broader economy. It remains to be seen whether it can perform consistently during different cycles. The recent return to profitability and triple-digit revenue growth are encouraging, and the valuation looks very appealing now. For investors who believe the economy will hold up, Upstart can be a Buy, but it’s a speculative bet, and it totally relies on your assumptions.

data.title: Lyft: A Strategic Partner For Robotaxis (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

I view Lyft, Inc. (NASDAQ:LYFT) as one of the key beneficiaries of the autonomous vehicle (AV) evolution or robotaxi. Ride-hailing companies, in my view, are in the sweet spot of an eventual proliferation of AV/robotaxis. Many AV developers and automakers are seeking JVs and partnerships to fast-track robotaxi deployment to monetize the years of R&D spending, as well as build assembly-line AVs. Lyft is a discounted stock with strategic value.

Tensor Robotaxi Simulation

Is a US$200k robotaxi a viable investment for a private investor using the Lyft network? The short answer is yes, with a few basic caveats. The first is that automobile vehicles are legal/regulated in most urban areas. Tensor/VinFast Auto (VFS), the car and AV technology manufacturer, needs a market to be feasible. Once the regulations are in place, a person could then purchase the US$200k robotaxi and hand it over to the Lyft network and wait for monthly checks.

Using Lyft's average trips and fare data (also corroborated by Uber (UBER)) and current take rate, I concluded that yes, the robotaxi owner could earn a 21% return on investment. This factors in US$200k for the car, US$12k a year in insurance, and US$10k for maintenance, with a useful life of 4 years.

Revenue is based on 20 trips per day at US$19 per trip for 350 days a year (15 days for repair). This generates about US$133k a year in gross revenue, with the robotaxi owners keeping 64% and Lyft 36%. If Tensor/Lyft reaches 100k fleet, it could double Lyft EBITDA. However, I would assume that there would be cannibalization, a loss or reduction in privately owned driven cars. Nonetheless, the theoretical productivity of an autonomous taxi fleet plugged into an established ride-hail network could be highly profitable even with an expensive car/infrastructure.

This is why Waymo, the most advanced robotaxi fleet, is partnering with Lyft and Uber. As I discussed in several reports, the ride-hail companies are in the sweet spot of an eventual robotaxi proliferation.

Current Consensus Forecast

The current consensus estimates have seen some uptick in growth and margins, with expectations of revenue growth driven by a combination of active drivers, trips, and take rates. While cash costs are largely increasing at a slower pace, the scale gains translate into higher margins that in turn produce outsized earnings growth and FCF. Lyft has turned GAAP net income positive in 2024 and should be able to generate over US$700 a year in FCF that may be used for buybacks and M&A.

Valuation

What is most attractive about Lyft is the still high valuation discount vs. its growth and also its peers. The consensus price target of US$19 went stale as Lyft engages in M&A, JVs, and partnerships to accelerate growth and position the company in a robotaxi ecosystem. I still see ample upside based on current estimates (ex robotaxi) and value the stock at 0.75x PEG with a US$30 price target for YE26.

Peer Comps

I used consensus data to compare Lyft to peers, competitors, and related companies. Uber is about twice Lyft in terms of a PEG ratio, while Tesla (TSLA), which supposedly will drive its future from the robotaxi (or is the robot), is more expensive. In my view, Lyft has a large target on it; both Tesla and Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) would greatly benefit from owning a ride-hailing network.

Risk

The main risk to Lyft is if Uber initiates a price war and cuts fares, which could hurt margin expansion. The other risks include regulations that reduce the ability to operate under the owner/operator business model. An increase in car costs, maintenance, and insurance is also a deterrent to increased drivers/revenue.

Conclusion

I rate LYFT a Strong Buy. This is a value stock with growth dynamics trading at under 0.5x PEG vs 25% EPS growth. It is also an M&A target for developing autonomous vehicle technology that can leverage Lyft's network at a very low entry cost.

data.title: GSK: Compelling Value, No Red Flags Ahead Of Earnings, Blenrep FDA Decision Soon
data.description:
Intro

When I conducted my analysis of Pfizer (PFE) a couple of weeks ago and performed the peer-to-peer relative valuation comparison, I noticed that while other so-called Big Pharmas were priced nearly the same using the one-year forward EV/EBITDA multiple, GSK plc aka GlaxoSmithKline (NYSE:GSK) stood out, as it was priced noticeably lower.

The pharma sector have been trading at higher levels since the Trump/Pfizer deal, alongside gains in GlaxoSmithKline.

While Pfizer and other pharma stocks have witnessed a pullback after the U.S. Administration deal, GSK has performed better, which could be largely attributed to the CEO transition, since its stock had already rallied over 3% before the Pfizer/Trump initiative was announced.

This relative undervaluation gained my attention, as Big Pharma is already priced quite conservatively, as I see it, and finding one at an even lower level raised my level of curiosity. Still remembered myself, there's no such thing as a free lunch. Usually, something is behind the scenes that causes the market to value a company relatively lower than its comparable peers. Finding the exact cause can sometimes be quite challenging, yet worth conducting as today’s business environment is highly dynamic and the narrative driving the undervaluation might just be on the verge of shifting.

Having conducted this analysis, I rate GSK as a Buy. The buy rating applies if the stock is trading at or below $45. If it rallies higher, I prefer to take a wait-and-see mode. However, the FDA action date for the key drug for growth perspective - Blenrep is 23 October 2025, and this may cause significant volatility. Investors should consider keeping their allocations accordingly.

Recent CEO change

At the time of choosing the theme of what to write an analysis on Monday, September 29, I briefly looked at Glaxo's ticker and noticed in the morning newsfeed that GSK's current CEO Emma Walmsley was stepping down after 9 years and will be replaced by the company's Chief Commercial Officer Luke Miels. I also saw that the share price got a nice +3% boost, and since the company's relative undervaluation was freshly on my mind, I decided to delve deeper into the roots of the GSK CEO change to understand why the market likes the dynamics here, and whether this could be the very cause of the stock's favorable valuation?

BioSpace offers its view:

With the departure of GSK CEO Emma Walmsley, the industry loses one of only two female leaders in charge of a top 20 pharma company—the other being Vertex’s Reshma Kewalramani.

Sounds to me we should be falling here, not to rally? As I understand, women at the helm nowadays are seen as positive catalysts, rather than the opposite.

Still, to gain more context, a quick rewind in time, the very same BioSpace summarizes back in 2021:

Elliott Management has been critical of Walmsley, who took over the CEO role in 2017, for her lack of experience with biopharma. Before taking over as CEO, Walmsley’s background with the company was managing GSK’s consumer healthcare division.

Walmsley spent 17 years at L’Oréal before coming to GSK, where she held several managerial positions, including overseeing the company’s consumer products division in China. Although she has surrounded herself with experienced leaders in the industry, such as Chief Scientific Officer and Head of R&D Hal Barron, Elliott Management has disputed her capabilities leading a profitable biopharma company.

For those who may not be aware, Elliott Management is one of the world's most respected activist hedge funds, known for its quite aggressive tactics to relentlessly pursue shareholders’ value. It is deeply engaging with underperforming companies to push major strategic, operational, or leadership changes. One of their most widely known cases is Twitter (now X), where it pushed to oust then-CEO Jack Dorsey.

Seems to me that not only Elliott, but markets broadly haven’t been really supportive of Emma Walmsley's leadership skills. As the saying goes, "numbers don’t lie," and markets tend to show their view through stock prices, which are, after all, just numbers.

As we can see on the GSK 10-year chart, I marked Walmsley's appointment in 2017 with blue, there hasn’t really been a very good relationship between Mr. Market and Emma Walmsley.

As markets look forward, I don’t delve further into the past since the leadership change is happening, and we’ve taken one reason off the table why markets haven’t treated GSK stock favorably. Let’s look into prospects ahead.

Brief Company Overview

While today's GlaxoSmithKline is a combination of many predecessors through mergers, acquisitions, and partnerships, the company itself dates its history back as far as 1715, with founding roots in London. While this long and proud history deserves a lot of attention, I won't rewrite it here; those who are interested can find an excellent overview on their homepage.

GSK today is a multinational company that employs approximately 70,000 people and is headquartered in London. Its main therapeutic areas are:

Recent Earnings

As of 1H of 2025, the revenue $20.18 billion, broken down as follows:

  • Specialty Medicines $8.14 billion 40.3% of total revenue
  • General Medicines $6.57 billion 32.7% of total revenue
  • Vaccines $5.44 billion 27% of total revenue

The main growth drivers were Specialty Medicines, growing +16% YOY:

During the second quarter, regional sales contributions showed that Europe was the fastest-growing area, seeing sales rise by 11%:

The company summarized its recent 2nd quarter performance:

The main double-digit growth drivers were within Specialty Medicines:

In summary, second-quarter earnings were solid, and management was upbeat regarding long-term prospects.

Lack of Blockbusters

Many Big Pharmas have some outstanding bestsellers, like Merck’s Keytruda, which makes up roughly half of the company's total sales. Pfizer has their Covid-19 vaccines franchise made just almost 40% of total revenue recently. It's really good to have these kinds of impressive blockbusters, but on the other hand, it also raises the level of risk as revenue is more concentrated in a few products. If their LOE or some other cause leads to sales disruptions, then it’s a serious headwind for the company.

While some observers point GSK has too few blockbusters, I personally like how their so-called eggs are nicely balanced in different baskets. GSK bestselling HIV products group, based on Dolutegravir, sold $3.48 billion in 1H2025, which made 17.3% of total sales. The second best-selling product was the Shingrix vaccine, which made $2.3 billion in revenue, accounting for 11.1% of total revenues, and third was Trelegy Ellipta, a COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease) and asthma drug, bringing in $2 billion, or 9.7% of the company's total sales. All in all, the top three products accounted for 38.1% of GSK's revenue. A fair point to mention, in my view, is that all three bestsellers belong to different categories, which is also a risk-reducing factor.

3rd quarter Earnings Preview

Since GlaxoSmithKline doesn't provide quarterly estimates, we must rely on their full-year estimates, which management revised slightly upward:

In my view, this signals management's strong confidence in their remaining prospects for the current year. As the pressure from the U.S. administration's tariff and MFN initiatives has also eased somewhat, I see no reason to doubt management's projections.

The Valuation

As I pointed out in the intro chapter, Glaxo notably shines out in peer-to-peer comparisons among fellow big pharmas, which indicates that GSK is relatively undervalued. While I don’t know what exactly is calculated inside the SA sector median multiples, since it shows much higher ratios than outright big pharma comparisons (it's probably all pharma sector, including medical devices, smaller biotechs, PBMs, etc.), it also reflects significant undervaluation as GSK Enterprise Value (EV) metrics are all well below the industry average.

It's important in my opinion that while many Big Pharma companies are projected to face substantial revenue gaps due to major Loss of Exclusivity (LOE) events between 2027 and 2029, GSK's revenue is estimated to grow during that same timeframe. Only the year 2030 is projected to show a slight drop in sales.

The major upcoming Loss of Exclusivity (LOE) problem is often blamed for the relatively low valuations of many Big Pharma companies. Given that, we should conclude that GSK might deserve higher multiples, not lower, compared to other big pharmas.

Since analysts are pointing to GSK's lack of exceptional blockbusters, the benefit of having a more differentiative portfolio is clearly reflected in its lower LOE hurdles in my view. This helps to ensure the company doesn't rely too heavily on a few hit drugs and doesn't get in trouble when these lose patent protection.

Additionally, the revenue generated by the GSK vaccine portfolio is not subject to the typical LOE cycles seen with other treatments, given the vaccine's absence of generics.

Furthermore, management consistently projects revenues of at least £40 billion ($53.2 billion) by 2031, signaling that the business is expected to return to growth starting around 2030.

To summarize here, I don’t see the reason why GSK should be valued lower than comparable pharmaceutical companies facing greater LOE risk. On the contrary, as I see it, GSK's lower risk profile deserves a comparatively higher valuation. This is the basis for my Buy rating.

Risks

While Glaxo faces all the typical risks to the pharmaceutical business – well summarized in their earnings announcements—I point out the three I view as most important:

  • CEO Transition: While there is currently no basis to suggest any specific risk here, the change in leadership always carries some.
  • U.S. Administration Initiatives: While immediate risks have eased after Pfizer/White House deal, Trump administration could surprise with further unexpected policy initiatives.
  • Key FDA Approvals: The successful approvals of depemokimab and Blenrep's new indications expected by the end of 2025—estimated to be multibillion-dollar opportunities—are critical for future growth.

Takeaway

While there are notable near-term risks, as the upcoming Blenrep FDA decision is just two weeks away, I view GSK plc stock as a solid investment. The company has a promising and balanced pipeline, a well-diversified portfolio of marketed products, strong financials, and limited Loss of Exclusivity headwinds.

After being in an underdog position and overlooked by the markets for years, I believe the combination of the CEO transition and underlying growth initiatives makes GSK a good choice if one considers investing in the pharmaceutical sector. It’s hard to see explosive returns here, but the attractive dividend yield and balanced risk profile offer a strong argument for more conservative investors.

data.title: Capcom: Spotlight On Peer's Privatization And Company's Game Pipeline
data.description:
I'm still positive on Capcom Co., Ltd. (OTCPK:CCOEY) (OTCPK:CCOEF) (9697:T), after assessing its mid-term prospects and its listed comparable's takeover.

My prior March 21, 2025, write-up drew attention to its opportunities in low-to-middle-income countries and the solid first-week sales of "Monster Hunter Wilds" or "MHW" game.

All Eyes Are On The Buyout Of CCOEY's Peer

SA News reported in late-Sep that "video game publisher Electronic Arts (EA)" will "go private in a historic $55 billion deal led by several private equity firms."

My take is that the recent event has favorable read-throughs for the stock. CCOEY could possibly be a M&A target in the future, just like EA.

Both of them have "Intellectual Property" Or "IP" that potential new owners value. EA is best known for its sports offerings such as "Madden NFL" and "EA Sports FC". In my March '25 update, I mentioned about Capcom's "Monster Hunter or MH Franchise" which continues to churn out new titles two decades after the initial launch.

There is no lack of potential suitors for Japan's gaming businesses. Microsoft (MSFT) had previously "eyed buying Square Enix, Sega" based on a mid-2023 Bloomberg piece.

Also, CCOEY's share price might be boosted by an expansion of its investor base, even if it stays listed.

Imagine you are a fund manager vested in EA. The acquisition is likely to have translated into gains. But one has to eventually re-deploy the funds elsewhere without distorting the portfolio's sector composition. Your choices have become more limited of late, given that another major player, Activision Blizzard, was also delisted in October 2023.

I believe that Japanese game-related stocks like CCOEY will be a beneficiary of fresh fund inflows, as institutional investors seek replacements for EA among their equity holdings.

Separately, the company's shares are trading below fair value, if I use this transaction as a reference.

BMO published a report (not publicly available) titled "EA Acquisition Analysis" on Sept. 29. The "deal valuation" was "22-23x LTM (Last Twelve Months) EV/EBITDA" according to the Canadian bank's estimates.

CCOEY's forward one-fiscal-year EBITDA multiple is 17.4 times, as per S&P Capital IQ data. This metric could possibly go up between +26% (22/17.4) and +32% (23/17.4), if one benchmarks against EA.

I'm Predicting Faster-Than-Anticipated Medium-Term Growth

CCOEY stressed at the FY25 (YE end-Mar) analyst briefing (S&P Capital IQ transcript) that "its goal is to achieve 10% annual operating profit growth," which it had "met for 10 consecutive years."

I see the group doing better with my projected FY2025-2028 "Operating Income" or "OI" CAGR of +15%. I'm excited about its pipeline.

The first ones to watch are "Resident Evil Requiem" and "Monster Hunter Stories 3," which are scheduled for launch in February and March next year, respectively.

Capcom's Key Franchises At A Glance

FY25 And All-Time Cumulative Sales ('000 Units) For Major Titles

As per the charts presented above, "Monster Hunter" and "Resident Evil" are among the most well-liked series within CCOEY's lineup. I think there's a good chance that the latest additions are popular and drive up the sale of older games.

According to Capcom's Aug '25 announcement, "Resident Evil Requiem" had "garnered high praise for its unprecedented sense of immersion" and "innovative gameplay" at "one of the largest European game awards (Gamescom)." This bodes well for this particular game's future sales performance.

Moving on to FY27-28, the spotlight is on the introduction of a brand-new IP called "Pragmata." It is in the "Science-Fiction Action-Adventure" category as per the company's website.

Bernstein issued a research note (inaccessible in public) with the title "Japan Video Gaming: The Beautiful Game" dated Sept. 30, 2025. The firm's analysts had visited the "Tokyo Game Show" or "TGS." They observed that the "crowd reaction towards Pragmata felt very encouraging" following long "queues for demo play."

There's always the concern that game developers are too reliant on their classic hits and don't put in sufficient effort to build new growth engines. It's still early days, but "Pragmata" has shown signs of promise to be another evergreen IP for the long run.

I expect CCOEY's EV/EBITDA to rise from the current high teens to the low 20s in line with EA's deal. The likely catalyst is robust profit growth driven by the new game cycle as outlined here.

You Should Contemplate These Bear-Case Scenarios

A valuation de-rating for Japanese equities or gaming enterprises due to macro- or industry-related headwinds would hurt Capcom's stock price.

CCOEY's upcoming launches might fail to gain favor with consumers. This can happen because of stiff competition or changes in gamer preferences.

My Closing Thoughts

I've stuck to my existing "Buy" rating for this name.

CCOEY has capital appreciation potential, considering its own intermediate-term outlook and the read-across from its peer's privatization. As I touched on earlier, its EV/EBITDA expansion upside is estimated to be +26%-32%.

Fast Facts

iShares Russell Top 200 Growth ETF (NYSEARCA:IWY) was launched on 9/22/2009 and tracks the Russell Top 200 Growth Index. IWY has a portfolio of 110 stocks, a 30-day SEC yield of 0.35% and an expense ratio of 0.20%. It is a very large and liquid ETF, with over $16 billion in assets under management (“AUM”) and an average daily dollar trading volume of $66 million. The fund’s issuer iShares is a part of BlackRock, Inc. (BLK).

Strategy

As described in the prospectus by iShares, the underlying index starts from the Russell Top 200 Index and selects companies with…

…higher price-to-book ratios, higher sales-per-share historical growth and higher forecasted growth relative to all securities included in the Russell Top 200 Index.

In summary, the fund focuses on very large growth companies. It is rebalanced on a quarterly basis, with a maximum limit of 22.5% in any constituent, and 45% in aggregate in those weighting more than 4.5%. The portfolio turnover rate was 28% in the most recent fiscal year and 11% in the previous year. This article will use as a benchmark the parent index, represented by iShares Russell Top 200 ETF (IWL).

data.title: The Prospects For NANO Nuclear Energy
data.description:
Crude oil, natural gas, coal, and biofuels are the traditional energy commodities. Nuclear energy has had its problems over the years, notably, Chernobyl and the Japanese Fukushima Daiichi accident caused many countries to abandon nuclear power solutions. However, in 2025, nuclear energy is back in vogue as the demand for power will surge over the coming years.

NANO Nuclear Energy Inc. (NASDAQ:NNE) shares have soared over the past few months, with prospects indicating even higher prices. NNE’s company profile states:

The company’s website highlights its role in the nuclear sector and its plans to become “the only vertically integrated nuclear fuel business of its kind in North America.” At $46.63 per share on October 10, NNE shares could have significant upside potential.

The demand for power is rising by leaps and bounds

Power, or electricity generation, can come from various sources. Crude oil, natural gas, and coal are traditional energy commodities that utilities use to generate electricity. While the United States employs a mix of energy sources, including natural gas, renewables, and coal, China and India depend heavily on coal.

Meanwhile, power demand is rising, with projected electricity demand expected to grow by 25% by 2030 and 78% by 2050 from 2023 levels. The U.S. power grid faces challenges in 2025 and will struggle to keep pace with demand. Increasing demand from electric vehicles, the growing number of data centers, and technological advances have put a tremendous strain on the U.S. power grid, making nuclear power generation a rising energy source for the future. While nuclear power has risks, it is a cleaner solution when compared to traditional fossil fuel inputs.

A bullish trend since the April 2025 low

At $49.18 per share, NANO Nuclear Energy Inc. had $1.97 billion in assets under management.

The daily year-to-date chart highlights NNE’s over 250% rally from the April 21 low of $17.26 to the October 7 high of $60.51 per share. On October 7, NNE reached a record high. While NNE shares pulled back to below $50 on October 10, the path of least resistance remains higher.

NNE is a liquid stock in a booming sector

With its market cap just below the $2 billion level, NNE shares trade an average of nearly 4.9 million shares daily, making it a highly liquid stock.

According to the World Nuclear Association, the United States is the world’s largest producer of nuclear power, producing approximately 30% of worldwide nuclear electricity.

In May 2025, President Donald Trump signed a series of executive orders titled “Reinvigorating the Nuclear Industrial Base, Reforming Nuclear Reactor Testing at the Department of Energy, and Ordering the Reform of the Nuclear Regulatory Commission.” The EO’s goal is to reestablish the United States as the global leader in nuclear energy by increasing capacity from 100 GWe to 400 GWe by 2050. The EO’s shorter-term goal orders the Department of Energy to prioritize work “with the nuclear energy industry to facilitate five gigawatts of power uprates to existing nuclear reactors and have ten new large reactors with complete designs under construction by 2030.” The executive orders provide financial incentives for the nuclear power industry.

The nuclear power sector is experiencing a boom, driven by the surge in AI data centers, growth in domestic manufacturing under the Trump administration, the continued electrification of vehicles and buildings, and the expansion of the oil and gas sector. One of the growing asset classes, cryptocurrencies, is a technological financial advancement. Crypto mining and blockchain technology increase electricity demand. All these factors put strains on the U.S. electricity grid, and nuclear power is a cleaner and efficient solution for the coming years.

Meanwhile, technology companies are investing billions in AI data centers, which only escalates power demand.

Mostly attractive Seeking Alpha Factor Grades - The reason to ignore profitability

NANO Nuclear Energy’s bullish path reflects the increasing demand for a U.S. nuclear power-generated grid solution.

Seeking Alpha’s Factor Grades for NNE award the highest A+ in growth and an A in the stock’s momentum, given the price action since the April 21, 2025, low. The D in revisions and failing grades in valuation and profitability reflect that NNE has posted losses over the past quarters since the stock began trading in May 2024.

There are many success stories of companies that posted losses while their shares rose before ultimately turning a profit.

An M&A candidate with a market capitalization below $2 billion

The critical factor supporting NNE and other companies in the nuclear power sector is the overwhelming power demand from leading technology companies, which are making substantial investments in artificial intelligence. AI demand from data centers will put a massive strain on the U.S. energy grid, and cash-rich tech companies could address their future energy requirements through equity investments or even acquisitions of utilities. At just under a $2 billion valuation, NNE could be an attractive takeover candidate for the nine companies with over $1 trillion valuations.

While nuclear energy solutions are likely to support energy companies, M&A activity from the technology sector could create a bidding war for companies like NANO Nuclear Energy Inc. I rate NNE a buy during the current dip that has taken the shares below the $50 per share level.

data.title: Baker Hughes: A Bet On Data Centers, Energy Infrastructure, And LNG
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Baker Hughes Company (NASDAQ:BKR) is one of the Big Three oilfield services companies, playing a vital role in the oil, gas and energy industries and going through significant changes and improvements over the past years, backed by solid macro developments and internal changes.

Right now, the market seems to be pricing in quite a lot of potential ahead, thanks to the industry’s tailwinds, backed by future AI and LNG demand, while the current environment could contradict these expectations and result in weaker spending on their customers’ side, potentially resulting in worse-than-expected results from BKR, so let’s take a deeper look.

The company delivered a very impressive Adjusted EBITDA margin improvement over the past half a decade, but I believe their ability to keep delivering that remains questionable. I say that because we have to keep in mind what happened during this time. This has been achieved through an amazing upcycle for oil and significant disruptions following the pandemic and the war in Ukraine.

Firstly, very importantly, following the war, the US began taking advantage of EU sanctioning and slowly cutting their dependency on Russia’s LNG. Even before Trump’s US-EU $750 billion LNG deal, the US was very quick to capitalize on this, with a very significant drop in the EU’s imports of Russian gas and a 119% jump in LNG imports from the US in 2022 compared to 2021. This fell by ~19% in 2023 and continued to grow in 2024, with the exports of US LNG to Europe (6.3 Bcf/d) still significantly higher than in 2021 (2.4 Bcf/d).

To this, add the fact that the very significant jump in oil prices refueled the industry’s investing activity, with record levels of CAPEX (and cash flows) reported by most oil and gas producers globally. This resulted in more investment activity, meaning a higher need for drilling, monitoring, well construction, and everything else that BKR does, which in turn allowed the company to invest in itself and develop alongside the industry.

Sure, their own actions also played an important role, we can’t deny that. BKR went through some significant portfolio optimizations over time, specializing even more in a rapidly developing environment and expanding their offerings into higher-margin areas (e.g., going for lower-carbon solutions over certain lower-margin oilfield solutions), with a massive recent acquisition that I’ll mention below being a good example of their portfolio rebalancing.

Still, we see a relatively weak first half in terms of cash flow from operating activities and free cash flow, although the second half tends to be the better one in terms of cash generation. Still, they expect the second half of the year to be better:

Following a strong first half and a positive outlook for the second half awards, we are confident in achieving IET's full year order guidance range of $12.5 billion to $14.5 billion. Looking beyond this year, we see continued momentum for power solutions, sustained growth in new energy, and a robust pipeline of LNG and gas infrastructure opportunities, all of which support a constructive outlook for orders.

Financially, based on their latest report, we see a very good position, with the current assets nearly covering the company’s current liabilities plus long-term debt. However, this will change significantly following a recent acquisition that I’ll mention in more detail below, although it will still remain solid for a company in its industry, and they plan to reduce their leverage significantly after the deal.

Their Q3 report is set to be released on October 23, and although I generally don’t give that much importance to a single quarter, what they’ll say in this one should be very important. We’ll get a potential update about the current environment and how they see the remainder of the year (with potential hints into how they see 2026), as well as details about the acquisition and other portfolio adjustments, on top of potential insights about industry tailwinds that could be useful for many other investments, so it’s definitely something worth paying a lot of attention to.

Dividends & Buybacks

BKR is paying a small ~1.9% dividend on top of ~1.6% in buybacks, which is significantly lagging behind most of the oil and gas or adjacent industries. As they say during the Q2 Earnings Report:

Our balance sheet remains in a very strong position. We ended the quarter with cash of $3.1 billion and net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 0.6x and liquidity of $6.1 billion. We also returned $423 million to shareholders. This included $227 million of dividends and $196 million in share repurchases. We remain committed to returning 60% to 80% of free cash flow to shareholders.

This is ultimately the result of the valuation, as even if they had a 100% payout ratio, the combined yield couldn’t sustainably be more than something below 5%, which is simply not good. This doesn’t seem like the type of investment to get for dividends and buybacks today, but rather for strategic importance and growth potential, in my opinion.

Risks & Opportunities

Rate cuts are probably the main near-term opportunity for BKR and their clients. Following the cut in September and with two more expected this year, we might be looking at significantly better costs of debt that could allow both the company and their clients to invest and develop once again. However, there’s also the risk of seeing rate cuts combined with tariffs and other issues lead to hard times for the US and global oil and gas trade, so this is a double-sided factor to keep an eye on.

They talked about the impact of tariffs, estimating a $100 million to $200 million hit to their consolidated EBITDA this year—assuming no further tariffs or retaliation, saying the following during their Q2 report:

So as we take all of these different variables and recognizing their dynamic, we have confidence in our mitigation actions that we put in place immediately. And we have, as you know, a flexible global supply chain. And so we maintain our previously communicated estimate of the $100 million to $200 million net EBITDA impact for the year. But just as a reminder, this does not -- this assumes the recently announced tariffs are implemented as scheduled, but it does not assume any further trade policy escalation, including retaliatory tariffs.

The company kept announcing various projects in recent months, such as supplying the liquefaction equipment for Sempra (SRE)’s Port Arthur LNG Phase 2, supplying up to 50 subsea trees for Petróleo Brasileiro S.A. - Petrobras (PBR) Brazil oil fields (plus a multi-year agreement with them), joining a partnership to develop one of the world’s largest geothermal projects, and many more.

They also announced the $13.6 billion acquisition of Chart Industries, Inc. (GTLS), whose shareholders recently approved it. The company generated about $4.2 billion in revenue and $1 billion in adjusted EBITDA in 2024, with 65 manufacturing locations globally, with BKR adding that this acquisition will help it expand its exposure to high-growth markets such as LNG, data centers and clean energy. This is a massive deal, and combined with the company’s recent developments mentioned above, it’s likely a reason why the stock performed well even in a weakening environment. Based on their estimations, BKR sees run-rate synergies growing from $95 million in year one to $230 million in year two and $325 million in year three thanks to SG&A optimizations, supply chain efficiencies and facility optimization, while commercial opportunities add further upside potential.

This highlights something very important about BKR: the company does much more than just help the oil industry. On top of the oilfield drilling, completions, etc., they also have significant exposure to natural gas, LNG, and energy, especially following this recent acquisition.

As I said before, I remain very bullish on LNG, and we’re currently looking at potential to see a “Third Wave” of LNG, with projects such as Golden Pass LNG, Plaquemines LNG (Phase 1 & 2), Corpus Christi LNG Stage III, Port Arthur LNG or Rio Grande LNG (Trains 1-3) more—potentially adding a lot more supply (potential for over 200 MTPA in new capacity).

This can then result in even more infrastructure needed, both for construction (which Baker Hughes already benefits from) and from the environment it would then create, so there’s definitely potential for BKR to see more growth ahead.

Plus, data centers, cloud and all the other tech that we’re referring to now will require massive amounts of energy and I remain bullish on that overall, and Baker Hughes has potential to develop from that significantly, especially if we see a rise in data centers near oilfields, such as the one that LandBridge Company LLC (LB) and NRG Energy, Inc. (NRG) are following a strategic collaboration to explore the development of a potential data center site in Texas' Delaware Basin. This could potentially offer a significant opportunity for BKR, although it’s still in a very early stage of development/emergence.

They actually see this as part of their “Horizon Two” market landscape, marked by gas, decarbonization and AI-led power demand surges. After that (2029 and beyond), they see growth coming from industrial and new energy markets, on top of the solid fundamental growth in the industry.

But still, oil prices remain very important for BKR at the end of the day. Yes, they aren’t a producer, but oil is falling to a point where oil and gas companies become forced to cut some CAPEX, and more pressure on its price for longer can result in less activity, fewer new projects, less drilling, etc., meaning a clear and direct hit to BKR’s revenues. Especially before the Chart acquisition, BKR had about 15% of its revenue coming from other industries than oil and gas, getting to ~30% following the deal. So, they are developing significantly beyond O&G, and oil itself is now taking a smaller and smaller stake in the company’s revenue mix.

What it means is BKR will also depend on things like OPEC’s decisions (recently announced another production hike for November) and political uncertainty—just see how they crashed ~20% in a couple of days back in April following Trump’s tariffs. However, as I mentioned in the TotalEnergies SE (TTE) analysis, the company projects that global oil supply (demand too) is expected to fall quite a bit in the medium term, especially now when virtually every oil producer is entering into a CAPEX-cutting territory due to the falling oil prices, which of course has an effect on the future supply, while other macro developments could continue to solidify the demand.

Regarding OPEC, they also commented during the latest earnings, saying that:

As we look into the second half of the year, we expect continued volatility as OPEC+ accelerates the return of its 2.2 million barrels per day of idle production into what we anticipate will be a soft market. Ultimately, until all excess OPEC+ barrels are absorbed by the market, we anticipate oil-related upstream spending will remain subdued.

As for BKR, they might display kind of a delay when it comes to weakness in oil, as projects aren’t cut overnight, so I believe it can take quite a bit until BKR would show it. This could also explain the current stock’s performance, other than LNG, and especially data centers potentially bringing some more demand for the stock, which might unfortunately fade quickly if they start reporting issues.

Still, if BKR can use this valuation to keep developing by acquiring peers that come at better valuations, this doesn’t have to be a bad thing. However, since Chart was an all-cash transaction despite the company’s very strong stock price, I highly question the management’s ability to take advantage of it. The acquisition was okay and makes sense from a strategic perspective, but using cash and significantly affecting the balance sheet is very questionable, although they say net leverage is projected to be 2.25x at close and plan to deleverage to 1.0-1.5x within 24 months.

So, a questionable move (buying with cash/debt over stock) but it doesn’t mean they’re bad. If we look at the bigger picture, we have a company that’s very much ready to take advantage of future trends, and with the surge in AI-related energy demand and beyond it, we’re likely looking at a lot of growth ahead for the industry.

Peer Comparison

Compared to the rest of the Big Three—SLB N.V. (SLB) and Halliburton Company (HAL)—as well as smaller and more specialized players like Weatherford International plc (WFRD), Tenaris S.A. (TS) and TechnipFMC plc (FTI), we see once again what I said throughout the analysis: a relatively high valuation compared to peers from most points of view.

Oddly enough, despite their focus on improving it, we see the weakest EBITDA margin (not adjusted) in the group and overall average profitability margins. Again, this valuation could be a tool that would allow them to buy said competitors and deliver significant synergies and per-share increases, assuming they want to issue stock to get them.

Valuation

For this valuation, I want to use the combined company from the beginning since the deal is very likely to go through. Starting with BKR’s last year’s free cash flow of $2.25 billion and Chart’s ~$390 million, we get to $2.64 billion in FCF in 2024. I’ll go for a 5% CAGR in the first 5 years, as well as $50 million in synergies per year (below their estimates since we can’t be sure how much is added to the cash flow, for a better margin of safety) for the first 3 years thanks to rate cuts and other industry tailwinds.

As a note, the company’s FCF/share grew at a CAGR of about 3.1% from 2015 to 2024—yes, only that from an industry slump into a good year, but let’s say they have significantly improved since 2020 on a fundamental basis.

After that, I’ll assume a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.75% terminal growth rate, plus a 8.5% WACC (assumptions: ~9.5% cost of equity, ~3.5% cost of debt), an estimated net debt following the acquisition of Chart of about $15.8 billion, as well as 985 million shares outstanding. This way, the Enterprise Value is $53.20 billion, and after deducting net debt, the equity value is $37.40 billion, leading to an intrinsic value per share of $37.97, slightly below the current level. And keep in mind that this doesn’t consider a potential downturn in case the environment results in less demand for their services.

Conclusions

Overall, I rate BKR a Hold now due to the little margin of safety and unattractive valuation, combined with the risk-reward it comes with at these prices. The company is fundamentally solid and should improve in the future, but the macro headwinds and internal risks remain, even if the industry has great long-term tailwinds and the company is adjusting itself to be able to capitalize on them better.

data.title: WTI Crude Steadies Near $61 As Traders Await Breakout From Tightening Triangle
data.description:
By Jainam Mehta

​WTI crude oil is trading around $61.3, consolidating near its critical support zone after a sharp pullback earlier this week. Price action has narrowed into a descending triangle structure, with a horizontal base around $61 and a series of lower highs pressing against declining resistance.

This tightening formation signals that a decisive breakout is approaching, leaving traders on edge as fresh catalysts loom.

Technical setup signals mounting pressure

From a technical standpoint, the $61 region remains a key inflection point. A clear break below this level would expose the next supports at $60 and $58.5, potentially extending the recent downtrend and confirming renewed seller dominance. On the upside, a rebound through $62.5–$63.1, where the 50- and 200-period EMAs converge, would mark the first sign of recovery. If momentum builds beyond this zone, targets around $65 and $67 could come back into play, levels that have repeatedly capped rallies since mid-September.

Momentum readings reflect this equilibrium between opposing forces. The RSI sits near 41, suggesting muted bullish drive as buyers struggle to regain control. Price continues to hover just below short-term EMAs, underlining the need for a firm defense of the $61 floor to prevent the structure from tilting decisively bearish.

Geopolitics and supply trends shape sentiment

Fundamental factors remain mixed. The recent progress in Israel-Hamas ceasefire negotiations has reduced the geopolitical risk premium that previously buoyed prices, triggering short-term selling. However, new U.S. sanctions targeting over 50 Iranian entities and vessels involved in energy trade have reintroduced supply-side uncertainty. Meanwhile, OPEC+’s restrained production hike indicates that producers remain cautious amid fragile demand forecasts.

According to EIA data, U.S. crude inventories rose for a second consecutive week, suggesting softer refinery demand. Yet, inventories at Cushing and across refined products declined, keeping overall market balances tight. This push-and-pull dynamic between geopolitical risk and inventory shifts continues to anchor oil within its current range, with traders awaiting the next major catalyst to define direction.

Outlook

Overall, WTI crude sits at a crucial juncture. A clean break below $61 would likely accelerate bearish momentum toward $58.5, while a successful defense could pave the way for another rebound toward $63–$65. The interplay between technical compression and evolving fundamentals—particularly developments in Middle East diplomacy, U.S. sanctions enforcement, and inventory trends—will likely dictate near-term volatility.

In earlier coverage, the $61–$61.5 region was highlighted as a key zone where short-term buyers had repeatedly defended against deeper losses. That support remains vital in determining whether oil can sustain its broader consolidation or extend into a more prolonged correction.

data.title: Ørsted: The ADR Is Going Up Significantly, And Here's Why
data.description:
Over the past few weeks, the ADR ticker for Ørsted A/S (OTCPK:DNNGY) has gone "gangbusters". By this, I mean that we've seen a very significant improvement in share price and returns. Usually, this would mean that I would take a "victory lap", and if you look at the way the stock has performed since my last article, I think you'd agree that a 6-7x outperformance of the SPY is actually a very good performance. You can see the outperformance from my last article below.

And if interested, you can find that article here.

However, note that the article title is not that I'm taking a victory lap. Nor do I think it would be suitable here. The reason is quite simple. The return or the change in share price we see in the ADR DNNGY is not reflected in the native ticker, ORSTED on the Copenhagen stock market. In the past month, as we can see on the ticker page on Seeking Alpha, DNNGY is up over 128%.

That is obviously impressive. However, the native ticker is not up even close to as much. In fact, the native ticker is down, from around 200 DKK to 122 DKK, and while we're up as of the time of writing this article, we're not seeing the sort of reaction for the native ticker that we're seeing for the ADR.

In this article, I will show you why the company seems to be recovering and also see why there is such a difference in native and ADR performance here.

Also, see what the company might be delivering in the next 1-3 years, and update my thesis, last covered in June of this year.

So let's see what we have here.

Ørsted—Upside is difficult to see, even with the recent trends considered.

So, a few things have happened since my last article. I won't go through the company's entire "fall from grace" in this piece. That has been extensively covered in the linked article above and others. Instead, we'll focus on relatively recent trends.

Despite everything that's happened, Ørsted is still a leader in wind power. With close to 10 gigawatts of gross offshore capacity, of which over 5 GW is net capacity as of year-end of the last complete fiscal, Ørsted is a leader in this. With over 80% of the company's EBITDA coming from these offshore farms, the company's business mix is unique, even amongst renewable players in Europe, which tend to be more diversified.

The issue is that much of Ørsted's previous upside was based on very generous subsidies from the European and other governments. Based on an average lifespan, I'll argue that these offshore wind farms that Ørsted has actually do not offer a very attractive lifespan—about 7 years for the ones the company has.

Subsidies have gone down. Not just in the USA, where the company in various projects is bearing the brunt of headwinds, but also in Europe. The company's entire strategy was based on, because of lower subsidies in Europe, expanding to the US with an ambitious 5GW in various offshore wind projects. At this point, we know how that went—it did not go well.

Between project halts, inability to find investors for projects like Sunrise Wind (Ørsted blamed hostile policies from US government, making investors wary of offshore wind), restructuring, massive strategic shifts, and ongoing legal action (never a risk neutral, regardless of how good your case is), it would be kind at this point to call the company's thesis "complex".

The reason the ADR is up, as I see it, is that investors are over-interpreting (or focusing) on company efficiencies, such as the recent news that the company is cutting 1/4 of its workforce, as well as raising $10B (close to) in a recent share sale. The fact that the Danish state actually subscribed to a full 50.1% of this issue should not be a surprise if you follow the trends and news. Like Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO), Ørsted is a bit of a "darling" for the Danish state—it's not going to abandon it.

That does not make the company a good investment, however.

The capital will be used to cover financing for the above-mentioned Sunrise, strengthening the capital structure. I believe this, together with the company's efficiency measures, as well as the rumors of a 50% sale of its UK-based offshore project, is responsible for the trends.

From a net perspective, we can now clearly state that the company is shoring up its balance sheet and significantly improving its fundamentals. However, it's doing so through dilution of original shareholders—and if the company were to sell part of its Hornsea 3 project, it's likely not at the price that investors expected the project to generate a few years back.

In fact, let me remind you that 2025 has not been a good year for Ørsted. Over 12B DKK of impairments booked in US projects alone so far, with further reductions to its net investment, tells a very clear story of the renewable market and the state of investments today—namely, that they are having trouble finding interested parties, further confirmed by the trends in Sunrise Wind.

It's a good idea when you're looking at risks for a company and a company doing a rights issue to see at what discount that company is doing the issue. When I first saw the issue, I did put some extra sugar in my coffee.

Why?

Because the 900M+ shares with dilution, with a share price of 66.6 DKK implied an over 65% discount to the latest closing share price.

Now, it turned out oversubscribed (another reason for the upside, I believe), but I believe this speaks volumes of the appeal, or rather lack of it, in this sector at this time. I made it very clear in my June article that Ørsted, while having some incredible "safeties" for the long term, comes at a very high risk factor. If you decided to swallow that risk/reward ratio and invest in DNNGY for an 80%+ outperformance of the SPY, then the only thing to do is to congratulate you on your superb short-term return.

However, I believe two things are crucial to understand at this point. First, Ørsted's earnings, no matter how much they're trying to dial down and fix the US, are tied to the US offshore. And because of President Donald Trump's and his administration's stance against wind, I would take fifteen steps back from any project in this sector unless I got it at "pennies on the dollar". I did expect the stop order against Revolution Wind in Connecticut to be lifted, as it was at the end of September of this year. But this is only one example, and more might be coming. I expect stop orders against projects like Sunrise. I do not doubt that A) Ørsted will beat them/have them lifted eventually, and B) The company now has the balance sheet to weather these orders.

But they will be materially impactful and dilutive on earnings—that much is clear to me.

Ørsted's positives can be stated with relative confidence—and I maintain that they are true. First, the rights issue is necessary and positive. While dilutive and at a discount, there really was no option. I like how oversubscribed it was as well. Secondly, interest rates continue to fall, and as a renewable player, Ørsted is favorably exposed to this. Thirdly, outside the US, the company's UK operations are doing well (a light point, actually), and once the indexation clauses (inflation-related) come into effect and remaining earnings stabilize, Ørsted could see some advantages.

But, as we'll see in risks later, there are a number of reasons right now why I believe that if you hold the ADR, you should consider rotating and taking home that profit.

Valuation and lack of upside for Ørsted going forward necessitate a cut in recommendation. I say "Hold" or rotate.

DNNGY is a 0.33x ADR: That means that the current value of the ADR, because it's a 0.33x ADR, implies that native Ørsted should trade at 468 DKK, based on current FX.

Does it?

No, Ørsted is trading at 122 DKK.

Less than a month ago, DNNGY was trading at less than half of the current value, and it would imply closer to 192 DKK, which by the way was also the level that Ørsted's native share traded at. It seems to me that the ADR has not been corrected for the rights issue, or there are other things at play here.

Whatever the case, however, the difference between DNNGY and Ørsted does not make sense at this time. I would also point out things like the recent S&P Global downgrade to BBB- for the company.

When it comes to the ADR, I only have one thing to say: get out. While this isn't a recommendation (I don't give stock "recommendations" as such), it's a very strong stance, which I will argue for in my risks and thesis below. If I owned a single share of the ADR, I would sell it.

The Ørsted native ticker trades at 17.8x P/E. The DNNGY ticker trades at almost 40x P/E. Native Ørsted can, at the current share price, be argued based on a 16-17% EPS growth annually over the next few years, to have an annualized P/Side of 13.3%. No dividend remains for the company at this time.

There is a case to be made for investing in Ørsted—at some point. I just don't believe that point to be now. The ADR DNNGY has gone through a spike in share price that I cannot say is "fair" or likely to be maintained, given the company's current trends. The native ticker Ørsted is fraught with uncertainty following a 115% dilution in shares after a massive rights issue that saw the share price dive from close to 200 DKK to below 120 DKK.

The main argument I make is that there are far better investment alternatives out there in the utility sector. Compared to another company with massive renewables, I would say SSE plc (OTCPK:SSEZY), which I invest in and which I believe has a significant upside.

If you wanted to go for Ørsted, I would put the company's target below 200 DKK. I do this cut from 300 DKK due to a 115% dilutive rights issue—and I could actually go lower and still be "in line" with my expected multiples. The discount was far, far above what I expected—my estimate was 30%. This was more than double that. I do not believe Ørsted has any moat left, and I expect a 2025E EBITDA of 23B DKK or thereabout, below the 24-27B DKK guidance. The UK will carry the growth, but I believe the USA will be more of a drag than the market expects.

I hold a negative view of Revolution and Sunrise in the US, which is why I cut growth estimates to 10-12% as opposed to 15-20% market expectations—and I forecast that Ørsted will need to continue to invest 150-190B DKK between the period of 2025-2029—and this includes both Sunrise Wind as well as the potential impacts of tariffs. Remember, this is not done either.

For that reason, I consider a fair value above 300 DKK to be speculative and a PT above 200 DKK to be the same. Even now, despite the fact that I technically have the company rated a "Buy" for the native, the covered ticker DNNGY is not a "Buy", because the current share price, coupled with the ADR multiple, puts it at a hold. A 200 DKK share price comes to a $10.5/share PT for DNNGY, and this makes it a "Hold".

Risks & Considerations

Risks to this company are, lacking a better word, significant. The company's rights issue, while absolutely necessary, is significantly dilutive. It has been described by some as a dilution simply to keep Sunrise Wind alive—and while not incorrect, it's also necessary. Still, it's worth noting that this is what the company has done. Just how dilutive was it?

The Number of shares for Ørsted increased by 115% based on 900M shares, which represents an EPS dilution of over 60%. Also, there are continued very significant political risks in the US, which is the company's by far most important geography. Even now that the order against Revolution Wind is lifted, the next risk is an order against Sunrise Wind.

And while interest rates are pointing down, there are plenty of indicators that they will not go down further. Any increase in overall interest would be a net negative to this company.

Thesis

Ørsted is now a heavily speculative renewables company that remains one of the relevant market leaders in the entire segment. It's state-owned, has a well-filled pipeline, and is one of the most conservative financing pipelines and maturities when it comes to utilities. But it's also exposed to some major market risks in the US, has had issues with its US-based projects, and recently completed a massively dilutive rights issue.

The ADR DNNGY has spiked to more than 2x the level it should be based on the multiple for the ADR, which is why I view this ADR as a rotation target with a "Hold" rating.

The upside is highly dependent on how the market for the offshore market develops and how the 2025-2029E period works out—I don't see a clear upside given multiple recent failures for the company, but the current price dictates this can be a "Buy". However, I would be looking at alternatives prior to investing here—and I say again, the "Buy" is for the native, not the ADR.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 2 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. Based on this and a price target of 200 DKK/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me, I give the company a "Buy" rating as of October 2025 for the native but a "Hold" for the ADR due to the discrepancies described herein.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
